saida_bruta:
As the boys climbed the hill, she noticed them pause and confer with one another. "You ask her, Niel."

"You do it, George. She's always at your place, and she hardly knows me well enough to speak."

When they stopped before the three steps leading to the front porch, Mrs. Forrester appeared at the doorway and nodded warmly, holding a pink rose in her hand.

"Good morning, boys. Heading out for a picnic?"

George Adams stepped forward and gravely removed his large straw hat. "Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. Could we please fish and wade in the marsh and eat our lunch in the grove?"

"Of course. What a lovely day for it. How long has school been out? Don't you miss it? I know Niel does. Judge Pommeroy tells me he's quite studious."

The boys laughed, and Niel looked embarrassed.

"Off you go, and make sure you don't leave the pasture gate open. Mr. Forrester can't stand having cattle wander onto his blue grass."

The boys walked quietly around the house toward the gate into the grove, then dashed shouting down the grassy slopes beneath the tall trees. Mrs. Forrester watched them from the kitchen window until they vanished behind the rise of the hill. She turned to her Bohemian cook.

"Mary, while you're baking this morning, make a pan of cookies for those boys. I'll bring them down while they're having lunch."

saida_bruta:
As the boys walked up the hill, she saw them pause and mutter to each other. "Go on, ask her, Niel."

"No, you ask, George. She's over at your place all the time, and she hardly knows me well enough to speak to."

When they arrived at the three steps leading up to the porch, Mrs. Forrester was standing in the doorway, nodding brightly, a pink rose in her hand.

"Good morning, boys. Heading out for a picnic, are you?"

George Adams came forward and gravely pulled off his big straw hat. "Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we fish and wade down in the marsh, and have our lunch up in the grove?"

"Certainly. What a fine day for it, too. How long has school been out now? Don't you miss it dreadfully? I know Niel does. Judge Pommeroy tells me he's quite the scholar."

The boys laughed, and Niel looked somewhat sheepish.

"Off with you now, and mind you don't leave the pasture gate open. Mr. Forrester can't bear having cattle get into his blue grass."

The boys crept quietly around the house to the gate that opened into the grove, then went tearing and shouting down the grassy slopes under the tall trees. Mrs. Forrester watched them from the kitchen window until they disappeared over the top of the hill. Then she turned back to her Bohemian cook.

"Mary, while you're baking this morning, put together a batch of cookies for those boys. I'll carry them down while the boys are having their lunch."

As the boys climbed the hill, she noticed them pause and murmur to each other. "Go ahead, ask her, Niel."

"No, you do it, George. She's always at your place, and she hardly knows me well enough to talk to."

When they reached the three porch steps, Mrs. Forrester stood in the doorway, nodding brightly, holding a pink rose.

"Good morning, boys. Heading off for a picnic, are you?"

George Adams stepped forward and gravely removed his big straw hat. "Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we fish and wade in the marsh, then have lunch up in the grove?"

"Of course. What a beautiful day for it. How long has school been out now? Don't you miss it dreadfully? I know Niel does. Judge Pommeroy tells me he's quite the scholar."

The boys laughed, and Niel looked a bit embarrassed.

"Off you go, then, and be careful not to leave the pasture gate open. Mr. Forrester can't bear having cattle wander into his blue grass."

The boys slipped quietly around the house to the gate leading into the grove, then ran and shouted down the grassy slopes beneath the tall trees. Mrs. Forrester watched from the kitchen window until they disappeared over the hilltop. Then she turned back to her Bohemian cook.

"Mary, while you're baking this morning, make a batch of cookies for those boys. I'll bring them down while they're having lunch."

saida_bruta:
As the boys walked up the hill, she saw them stop and whisper to one another. "Go on, ask her, Niel."

"No, you ask, George. She's always at your house, and she barely knows me well enough to speak to."

When they arrived at the three porch steps, Mrs. Forrester was standing in the doorway, nodding cheerfully, a pink rose in her hand.

"Good morning, boys. Off for a picnic, are you?"

George Adams stepped forward and solemnly took off his big straw hat. "Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. Might we fish and wade in the marsh, then eat lunch up in the grove?"

"Certainly. What a lovely day for it. How long has school been out already? Don't you miss it terribly? I know Niel does. Judge Pommeroy tells me he's quite the student."

The boys laughed, and Niel looked slightly embarrassed.

"Run along, then, and be sure not to leave the pasture gate open. Mr. Forrester can't stand having cattle stray into his blue grass."

The boys slipped quietly around the house to the gate leading into the grove, then dashed and hollered down the grassy slopes beneath the tall trees. Mrs. Forrester watched from the kitchen window until they vanished over the hilltop. Then she turned back to her Bohemian cook.

"Mary, while you're baking this morning, make a batch of cookies for those boys. I'll take them down while they're eating lunch."
